facts.t: Thomas Jefferson and John Adams both died on July 4, 1826, the 50th anniversary of the Declaration of Independence.
The American Temperance Society formed in Boston in 1826, launching the first national anti-alcohol crusade.
James Fenimore Cooper published his frontier novel The Last of the Mohicans in 1826.
Freemason William Morgan vanished in 1826 after threatening to expose Masonic secrets, sparking the Anti-Masonic Party.
The Erie Canal, finished in 1825, had its first full year of booming trade between the Great Lakes and the Atlantic in 1826.
New York State completed the total abolition of slavery on July 4, 1827.
Freedom's Journal launched in New York City in 1827, the first newspaper owned and run by Black Americans.
New Orleans recorded its first documented street maskers parading for Mardi Gras in 1827.
James Fenimore Cooper published The Prairie, the third of his Leatherstocking novels, in 1827.
A schooner loaded with animals was sent plunging over Niagara Falls in 1827 as a publicity stunt for gawking crowds.
Andrew Jackson swept the 1828 election, ushering in an era of populist mass politics.
Congress passed the punishing Tariff of 1828, which Southerners bitterly nicknamed the Tariff of Abominations.
Noah Webster published his American Dictionary of the English Language in 1828, standardizing American spelling.
Construction broke ground on the Baltimore and Ohio Railroad in 1828, America's first common-carrier railroad.
The Cherokee Phoenix began publishing in 1828 in Georgia, the first Native American newspaper in the country.
At Andrew Jackson's chaotic 1829 inauguration reception, staff lured a mob out of the White House with spiked punch on the lawn.
William Austin Burt patented his typographer in 1829, an early American precursor to the typewriter.
New York's Working Men's Party formed in 1829, among the earliest US labor political movements.
David Walker published his fiery anti-slavery pamphlet Walker's Appeal in Boston in 1829.
Boston's Tremont House opened in 1829, often called America's first modern luxury hotel with indoor plumbing.
President Andrew Jackson signed the Indian Removal Act into law in 1830.
The tiny steam locomotive Tom Thumb raced a horse-drawn car on B&O rails in 1830 and famously lost to a mechanical failure.
Joseph Smith published the Book of Mormon and organized his church in 1830.
The 1830 census counted about 12.8 million Americans, up nearly a third from a decade earlier.
Around 1830, American per-capita hard-liquor drinking hit the highest level in the nation's history, historians estimate.
Nat Turner led a slave rebellion in Southampton County, Virginia, in August 1831.
William Lloyd Garrison began printing his abolitionist paper The Liberator in Boston in 1831.
Cyrus McCormick demonstrated his horse-drawn mechanical reaper in Virginia in 1831.
The patriotic hymn My Country, 'Tis of Thee was first sung publicly in Boston on July 4, 1831.
The New York and Harlem Railroad was chartered in 1831, an ancestor of today's New York City transit system.
Andrew Jackson vetoed the re-charter of the Second Bank of the United States in 1832, igniting the Bank War.
The Black Hawk War was fought across Illinois and Wisconsin Territory in 1832.
Democrats held their first national nominating convention in Baltimore in 1832, renominating Andrew Jackson.
A cholera pandemic swept American port cities in 1832, killing thousands in New York alone.
The New York and Harlem Railroad opened America's first horse-drawn city streetcar line in 1832.
Abolitionists founded the American Anti-Slavery Society in Philadelphia in December 1833.
Oberlin College was founded in Ohio in 1833 and became the first US college to regularly admit Black students and women.
A dazzling Leonid meteor storm lit up American skies in November 1833, convincing many onlookers the world was ending.
The New York Sun launched in 1833 as one of the country's first cheap penny newspapers.
Chicago was officially incorporated as a town in 1833, when its population was barely 200 people.
Opponents of Andrew Jackson organized the Whig Party in 1834.
Cyrus McCormick won a US patent on his mechanical reaper in 1834, revolutionizing American wheat harvesting.
An anti-Catholic mob burned the Ursuline convent in Charlestown, Massachusetts, in 1834.
Frontiersman Davy Crockett published his colorful autobiography in 1834, cementing his folk-hero fame.
Female mill workers in Lowell, Massachusetts, staged one of America's first organized factory strikes in 1834.
Richard Lawrence tried to assassinate President Andrew Jackson in 1835, but both his pistols misfired.
The Texas Revolution erupted in 1835 when settlers clashed with Mexican troops at the Battle of Gonzales.
Texas settlers formally organized the Texas Rangers as a militia force in 1835.
Showman P.T. Barnum launched his career in 1835 by exhibiting an elderly enslaved woman he falsely billed as 161 years old.
A massive fire destroyed much of Lower Manhattan's financial district in December 1835.
Texans declared independence and fought the Battle of the Alamo in 1836.
Arkansas joined the Union as the 25th state in June 1836.
McGuffey's Eclectic Readers debuted in 1836 and went on to teach generations of American schoolchildren.
Congress passed the 1836 gag rule, automatically tabling every anti-slavery petition sent to the House.
Samuel Colt received his US patent for the revolving-cylinder pistol in 1836.
The Panic of 1837 struck, plunging the United States into a years-long economic depression.
Michigan entered the Union as the 26th state in January 1837.
Chicago was upgraded from town to incorporated city in 1837.
Blacksmith John Deere forged his first successful steel plow in Illinois in 1837.
A pro-slavery mob murdered abolitionist newspaper editor Elijah Lovejoy in Alton, Illinois, in 1837.
The US Army forced thousands of Cherokee onto the deadly Trail of Tears beginning in 1838.
A bloodless border dispute between Maine and New Brunswick, the Aroostook War, flared up in 1838.
The steamship Great Western reached New York from Britain in 1838, among the first transatlantic steam crossings.
Frederick Douglass escaped from slavery in Maryland in 1838 disguised as a sailor.
Congress organized the Iowa Territory in 1838.
Enslaved Africans aboard the schooner La Amistad revolted in 1839, and the ship was seized off Long Island.
A Boston newspaper first printed the joke abbreviation O.K. in 1839, launching America's favorite two-letter word.
Legend later credited Abner Doubleday with inventing baseball in Cooperstown, New York, in 1839, a story historians now call a myth.
Charles Goodyear accidentally discovered rubber vulcanization in 1839 after dropping a rubber-sulfur mix on a hot stove.
News of the newly invented daguerreotype photograph reached America in 1839, sparking an instant photography craze.
William Henry Harrison won the presidency in 1840 in the raucous Log Cabin and Hard Cider campaign.
Abolitionists founded the Liberty Party in 1840, the first US political party built solely around ending slavery.
The US Exploring Expedition confirmed Antarctica was a continent in 1840.
Congress created the Independent Treasury system in 1840 to separate federal funds from private banks.
The 1840 census counted just over 17 million Americans.
President William Henry Harrison died just 31 days into office in 1841, and John Tyler became the first VP to succeed a dead president.
Rhode Island's Dorr Rebellion began brewing in 1841 over who had the right to vote.
The Supreme Court ruled in 1841 that the Africans aboard the Amistad had been illegally enslaved and were free.
The Bartleson-Bidwell Party set out from Missouri in 1841, the first organized wagon train bound for California.
P.T. Barnum bought a rundown New York museum in 1841 and turned it into his famous American Museum.
The Webster-Ashburton Treaty settled the long US-Canada border dispute over Maine in 1842.
Rhode Island's Dorr Rebellion reached its armed showdown in 1842 over voting rights.
Massachusetts's top court ruled in Commonwealth v. Hunt in 1842 that labor unions were not automatically illegal.
John C. Fremont led his first western expedition in 1842, mapping routes toward the Rockies.
The Washingtonian temperance movement, founded by reformed drinkers, swept American cities in the early 1840s.
Nearly a thousand settlers set out on the Oregon Trail's first mass wagon train in 1843, known as the Great Migration.
Reformer Dorothea Dix presented a shocking 1843 report to Massachusetts lawmakers on the abuse of the mentally ill.
John C. Fremont set out on his second expedition in 1843, mapping much of the Oregon Trail and Great Basin.
P.T. Barnum's tiny performer General Tom Thumb became a national sensation starting in 1843.
Thousands of American Millerites sold their belongings in 1843, convinced preacher William Miller's math proved the world was ending.
Samuel Morse sent his first long-distance telegraph message, "What hath God wrought," from Washington to Baltimore in 1844.
James K. Polk won the 1844 presidency on an aggressively expansionist platform.
Miller's followers faced the Great Disappointment on October 22, 1844, when their predicted end of the world never came.
Anti-Catholic mobs rioted in Philadelphia in 1844, burning churches in weeks of deadly nativist violence.
A mob murdered Mormon founder Joseph Smith in Carthage, Illinois, in 1844.
Texas was annexed and admitted to the Union as the 28th state in 1845.
Journalist John O'Sullivan coined the phrase Manifest Destiny in 1845 to justify American westward expansion.
Florida became the 27th state in March 1845.
The Irish Potato Famine began in 1845, soon sending a huge wave of immigrants to American shores.
Edgar Allan Poe published The Raven in January 1845, becoming an overnight literary sensation.
The Mexican-American War broke out in 1846 after a border clash along the Rio Grande.
The Oregon Treaty of 1846 fixed the US-British boundary at the 49th parallel, ending the "54-40 or fight" showdown.
The ill-fated Donner Party set out for California in 1846, later becoming trapped by snow in the Sierra Nevada.
Elias Howe patented an early lockstitch sewing machine in 1846.
Congress chartered the Smithsonian Institution in 1846 with a British scientist's surprise bequest.
Brigham Young led the first Mormon pioneers into the Salt Lake Valley in July 1847.
The United States issued its first adhesive postage stamps in 1847, featuring Franklin and Washington.
US forces captured Mexico City in September 1847, a turning point in the Mexican-American War.
Frederick Douglass launched his own abolitionist newspaper, The North Star, in Rochester in 1847.
Cyrus McCormick opened his reaper factory in Chicago in 1847, the seed of what became International Harvester.
The Seneca Falls Convention, the first US women's rights convention, met in July 1848.
The Treaty of Guadalupe Hidalgo ended the Mexican-American War in 1848, ceding vast western lands to the US.
Gold was discovered at Sutter's Mill, California, in January 1848, setting off the Gold Rush.
Wisconsin joined the Union as the 30th state in May 1848.
Stephen Foster published the song Oh! Susanna in 1848, which became the unofficial anthem of the Gold Rush.
Tens of thousands of "forty-niners" rushed to California in 1849 chasing gold.
Elizabeth Blackwell became the first woman to earn a medical degree in the United States in 1849.
Harriet Tubman escaped slavery in Maryland in 1849 and later became the Underground Railroad's most famous conductor.
Congress established the US Department of the Interior in 1849.
Edgar Allan Poe died in Baltimore in October 1849 under mysterious circumstances that remain unexplained.
The Compromise of 1850 admitted California as a free state and imposed a harsh new Fugitive Slave Act.
California entered the Union as the 31st state in September 1850.
Nathaniel Hawthorne published The Scarlet Letter in 1850.
President Zachary Taylor fell ill after a scorching July 4, 1850 ceremony and died five days later.
The 1850 census was the first to record every free American by name rather than just heads of household.
